text: I'd like to talk in this video about a suturing technique we use for robotic ventral hernia while sewing in mesh. I like to call it the sewing machine technique. Others have called it the dolphin technique. But in either event, the needle goes in and comes right back out of the mesh, and we're going to go in a straight line rather than over the edge.
But as you can see, we often start out in our learning curve sewing kind of like you just saw there, with a whole lot of different grabs on the needle.

So if you actually count them here, you'll see that there's one here and then we're gonna take our other hand and kind of pull it all the way through and then take two or three more times to reposition it in the hand that's sewing.

The surgeon uses a robotic suturing technique on ventral hernia mesh, keeping the needle straight and minimizing grabs. The non-dominant hand pulls the needle straight out of the tissue, unwinds it, and hands it back to the dominant hand on the left for continued sewing.

Another key here is with your non-dominant hand, pulling it all the way through and leaving room on the needle where you can grab it with your dominant hand about three quarters of the way back. A lot of people grab it kind of in the middle of the needle and you won't get the needle all the way through. You'll have to let go and re-grab and push it.
So there you have it suturing the mesh made simple, at least a little more simple.
And if you'd like any more information on this or other robotic techniques and things, you can visit clarkgerhart.com.
